evidence_basis_summary: Paper describes purposeful stakeholder sampling criteria (school level, geography, role, student demographics) but the study does not frame this as an AI-contestable relevance decision; no corpus/literature screening is involved.
notes: Upgraded low to medium: arXiv preprint (C005_S1) makes the sampling logic explicit enough to judge this role as not the analytic pressure point.

evidence_basis_summary: Study is a thematic and sentiment coding comparison (GPT-4 vs human vs LDA/VADER); no causal mechanism claims are adjudicated, so this role is confidently not applicable.
notes: Upgraded low to high: full text confirms the paper is classificatory rather than causal-mechanistic.

evidence_basis_summary: Three expert coders iteratively built a Resource Equity codebook from grounded reading of 10 interviews and then used it to discipline GPT-4 outputs; humans retain boundary-setting authority while AI does the labeling.
notes: Upgraded medium to high: section 5.1 (Codebook Development) directly describes expert-led construct definition around themes such as governance, anti-racism, data access.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors deliberately retain LDA, lexical VADER, GPT-4, and human coding side by side rather than collapsing onto one machine-friendly pipeline, but the corpus itself is a single WA interview set.
notes: Upgraded low to medium and flipped observable to yes: multi-method triangulation in sections 2.2 and 5 preserves methodological pluralism even though corpus pluralism across sources is limited.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper documents an explicit workflow (Fig. 2), iterative prompt design and evaluation, LDA validation rubrics in Appendix A.2, and cross-validation steps; humans architect the admissible procedure around GPT-4.
notes: Upgraded medium to high: methods section and workflow diagram make the protocol/provenance work directly visible.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors flag that Cohen's kappa drops sharply on data unseen by GPT during training and that domain specificity limits generalization, signalling a transportability concern even though the study stays within one state corpus.
notes: Upgraded low to medium and flipped observable to partial: related-work discussion of Dai et al. (Cohen's kappa dropping to 0.34 on unseen data) explicitly surfaces transportability risk.

evidence_basis_summary: Human interviewers conducted 24 semi-structured Zoom interviews with policy stakeholders and research assistants manually corrected transcripts; situated human presence is what generates the data and cleans the tacit features GPT-4 cannot recover.
notes: Upgraded medium to high: sections 4.1 and 4.2 give direct evidence of interviewer and transcript-cleaning work.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors frame GPT-4 as an adjunct to human experts in high-stake, context-dependent policy decisions and caution against black-box deployment, but authorship, disclosure, and responsibility allocation are not the paper's central focus.
notes: Upgraded low to medium and flipped observable to partial: introduction and section 2.1 invoke accountability/bias concerns without making this the analytical crux.

evidence_basis_summary: Expert coders define and stabilize theme boundaries (e.g., governance, anti-racism, data accessibility) in the Resource Equity codebook before GPT-4 is allowed to apply them.
notes: Upgraded medium to high: direct evidence in section 5.1.1 of expert-driven category definition.

evidence_basis_summary: Purposeful sampling across race/ethnicity, SES, geography, and professional role preserves subgroup heterogeneity, and findings report theme prominence varying by stakeholder job role.
notes: Upgraded low to medium and flipped observable from partial to yes: section 4.1 sampling design and results tying themes to job roles provide concrete evidence.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper references prior work showing large kappa drops on unseen data and warns against over-generalizing GPT-4 coding to new domains, but does not itself run a transportability test.
notes: Upgraded low to medium and flipped observable to partial: transportability is discussed as caveat rather than tested.

evidence_basis_summary: Authors iteratively tune prompts and benchmark GPT-4 against human expert coding and VADER/LDA reference distributions, matching a calibration-architect pattern at the prompt-evaluation stage.
notes: Upgraded low to medium and flipped observable to partial: prompt iteration and benchmarking in abstract and method sections behave like calibration work, though not framed as simulator calibration.

evidence_basis_summary: Human interviewers conducted Zoom interviews with diverse policy stakeholders, shaping what respondents disclosed and how the interaction unfolded.
notes: Upgraded low to high and flipped observable to yes: section 4.1 directly documents the interviewer role.

evidence_basis_summary: Research assistants meticulously listened to audio to correct transcripts and paragraph boundaries, supplying tacit contextual judgement that GPT-4 could not provide.
notes: Upgraded medium to high: section 4.2 provides direct evidence of context-witness work on transcripts.

evidence_basis_summary: Study involves no title/abstract triage or coarse study-type classification; it codes interview paragraphs against an expert codebook.
notes: Upgraded low to high: full text confirms this role is simply not part of the workflow.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: GPT-4 matches human coding at 77.89% for specific themes and 96.02% for broader themes and aligns well on sentiment; humans plausibly shift from primary line-level coders toward auditors of machine labels within the expert codebook.
notes: Upgraded low to high and status from conditional to shrinking: reported congruence figures in the abstract and MRQ1 results directly evidence the shrink toward auditing on consensus-rich coding.

evidence_basis_summary: GPT-4 is used for thematic and sentiment coding of interview text, not for drafting or polishing the research paper's prose.
notes: Upgraded low to high: no evidence in the paper of AI-assisted prose drafting.
